title: Making a hump mould for bowls
subtitle: How to set up a dome that holds a slab in shape while it stiffens
excerpt: A hump mould lets a flat slab of clay drape over a convex form and stiffen into a consistent bowl or plate shape without the clay collapsing. The mould itself is any smooth dome you have to hand: an upturned bowl, a ball of crumpled newspaper, or a dedicated plaster dome.
sourceNotes: Hump mould technique for hand-building is a standard pottery forming method. Encyclopaedia Britannica, 11th edition (1911), ceramics entry. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A convex dome form over which a slab of clay is draped. The clay conforms to the dome's shape as it stiffens, producing a consistent bowl or plate.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim and join.
A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness.

aliases: how to make a bowl mould

content.text: A
hump mould
is the simplest way to produce a consistent bowl or plate from a rolled
slab

without a wheel. The slab drapes over the dome, gravity does most of the shaping, and the clay stiffens in that form. The mould is removed before the clay reaches leather-hard, so the piece can be trimmed and refined on the bench.
What works as a mould
Upturned ceramic or glass bowl: the most reliable option. The smooth exterior makes a smooth interior surface on the clay. Choose a bowl whose bottom profile matches the curve you want.
Inflated balloon: gives a symmetrical dome for round bowls. Deflate slowly once the clay is leather-hard.
Crumpled newspaper ball: useful for irregular organic forms. Remove the paper once the clay stiffens enough to hold its shape.
Preparing the mould

Cover the mould with a single piece of thin fabric (an old tea towel or muslin square) or a sheet of plastic. This prevents the clay from sticking and allows you to lift the clay off the mould cleanly. For a very smooth interior surface, use plastic rather than fabric; for a slightly textured interior, use fabric.
Step-by-step
1. Roll the slab

Roll the clay to an even 6 mm thickness using guide rails. A 200 g piece of air-dry clay produces a slab large enough for a 15 cm bowl. Let the slab rest for 5 minutes on the board before picking it up; this allows the surface stress from rolling to equalise and reduces tearing.
2. Drape over the mould

Lift the slab carefully on both palms and lay it over the centre of the prepared mould dome. Let it fall naturally. Do not press it onto the mould: pressed clay will show the mould texture and may crack as the clay shrinks away from the rigid surface. Let gravity drape the slab.
3. Smooth and trim
Use a wooden rib to lightly smooth the surface and remove any wrinkles. Trim around the edge with a fettling knife to the bowl's intended diameter. The cut edge will form the rim.
4. Wait for leather-hard
Leave the clay on the mould until it reaches
leather-hard
. At leather-hard, the clay holds the bowl shape without support. Lift the clay bowl off the mould by gripping the fabric or plastic underneath and inverting gently. The mould should release cleanly.
5. Refine the rim

Set the bowl upright. Trim the rim level if needed with a fettling knife or smooth it with a damp sponge. At leather-hard stage the rim trims cleanly without distorting. Smooth joins on the exterior if the clay folded at the edge during draping.
What it looks like when it is right
The lifted bowl holds its curve without drooping. The interior surface is smooth where the clay rested against the mould cover. The wall thickness is even throughout at about 6 mm.

items.cause: No fabric or plastic barrier between clay and mould
items.fix: Always interpose a thin fabric or plastic sheet; remove the mould before the clay is fully bone-dry

items.symptom: Slab tears when lifted onto the mould
items.cause: Slab is too thin or clay is too soft
items.fix: Roll to at least 6 mm; rest the slab for 5 minutes before picking up

items.symptom: Bowl rim cracks as it dries
items.cause: Rim dried faster than the base of the bowl
items.fix: Cover the rim with a narrow strip of plastic while the rest of the bowl finishes drying
